title: [Seminar]˝A Formal but Pragmatic Foundation for General-Purpose Operating Systems˝ by Timothy Roscoe (EPFL) on Oct 17th.
content: Seminar Announcement Title: A Formal but Pragmatic Foundation for General-Purpose Operating Systems Speaker: Prof. Timothy Roscoe (ETH Zurich) Date & Time: [Insert date and time] Venue: [Insert location] Host: School of Computing, KAIST Abstract Operating Systems (OS) are fundamental to the correct operation of all non-trivial computing systems. General-purpose OSes such as Linux (and Android), Windows, iOS, and macOS form the backbone of modern computing and communication infrastructure — from mobile devices to large-scale cloud platforms. Modern AI systems would not be feasible without the scaling and inter-task communication capabilities provided by OS software. However, OS flaws can have severe consequences, ranging from security breaches to global outages. Despite their critical role, the design and implementation of today’s general-purpose OSes remain surprisingly ad hoc, built on outdated architectures conceived for machines of the 1970s. Modern hardware has evolved dramatically: computers are now complex networks of cores, devices, management engines, and accelerators, much of which operates beyond the traditional OS’s visibility. This growing disconnect between hardware reality and OS structure is a key source of security and reliability problems and demands a fundamentally new approach. In this talk, I will present our efforts to establish a rigorous foundation for general-purpose OS development, grounded in a formal framework that captures the software-visible semantics of all hardware components in complete, real-world systems. Building upon this, we are developing tooling to construct OSes for modern heterogeneous servers and systems-on-chip that can integrate existing drivers, firmware, and application environments while still providing strong, formal guarantees of platform-wide isolation and security. Speaker Bio Timothy Roscoe is a Full Professor in the Systems Group of the Department of Computer Science at ETH Zurich, where he works on operating systems, networks, and distributed systems. He received his PhD in 1995 from the University of Cambridge, where he was a principal designer and builder of the Nemesis OS. After working on web-based collaboration systems at a startup in North Carolina, he joined Sprint’s Advanced Technology Lab in California in 1998, focusing on cloud computing and network monitoring. In 2002, he joined Intel Research Berkeley as a principal architect of PlanetLab, an open, shared platform for deploying planetary-scale services. Since joining ETH Zurich in 2007, Prof. Roscoe has led research on the Barrelfish multikernel OS, distributed stream processors, and formal specifications of hardware/software interfaces. He was named an ACM Fellow in 2013 for his contributions to operating systems and networking research. His current work centers on formal methodologies for OS design and Enzian, a powerful hybrid CPU/FPGA machine for systems research.
